repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem:

vim.iter annotations were too opaque, and the generated help did not
explain generic Iter and ArrayIter types clearly.

Solution:

Add explicit generics to Iter and ArrayIter, tighten the annotations for
array-only methods and multi-value pipelines, and update the luacats
parser, vimdoc generation, and tests so the generated help preserves
those types.

AI-assisted: Codex

comment_data.body: do we need to mention all these  or just one or 2
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -6,22 +6,28 @@
 --- of each pipeline stage is input to the next stage. The first stage depends on the type passed to
 --- `vim.iter()`:
 ---
---- - Lists or arrays (|lua-list|) yield only the value of each element.
+--- - Lists (|lua-list|) yield only the value of each element.
 ---   - Holes (nil values) are allowed (but discarded).
----   - Use pairs() to treat array/list tables as dicts (preserve holes and non-contiguous integer
----     keys): `vim.iter(pairs(…))`.
+---   - Use pairs() to preserve holes and non-contiguous integer keys: `vim.iter(pairs(…))`.
 ---   - Use |Iter:enumerate()| to also pass the index to the next stage.
 ---     - Or initialize with ipairs(): `vim.iter(ipairs(…))`.
+---   - List input returns an ArrayIter, which supports list-only methods such as
+---     |ArrayIter:rev()|, |ArrayIter:flatten()|, |ArrayIter:pop()|, |ArrayIter:rfind()|,
+---     |ArrayIter:rpeek()|, |ArrayIter:rskip()|, and |ArrayIter:slice()|.

comment_data.body: this kind of thing isn't needed since https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/36205 . Would prefer not to duplicate this everywhere. `K` on the existing `(vim.Diagnostic[])` should "just work".
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -2255,7 +2256,7 @@ from({diagnostics})                                *vim.lsp.diagnostic.from()*
     Converts the input `vim.Diagnostic`s to LSP diagnostics.
 
     Parameters: ~
-      • {diagnostics}  (`vim.Diagnostic[]`)
+      • {diagnostics}  (`vim.Diagnostic[]`) See |vim.Diagnostic|.

@@ -16,7 +69,9 @@ vim.func = require('vim.func')
 vim.glob = require('vim.glob')
 vim.health = require('vim.health')
 vim.hl = require('vim.hl')
-vim.iter = require('vim.iter')
+local iter = require('vim.iter')
+---@cast iter vim.IterModule

comment_data.body: how about naming it `vim.IterArray` , for discoverability?

idk what's going on here. this is a bit overwhelming; is this section telling users anything that isn't covered by the main `iter` section?

the "ArrayIter" section above I suppose makes sense, but maybe we don't need this one.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3009,16 +3017,226 @@ Examples: >lua
 <
 
 
+vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...> *vim.ArrayIter*
+    Extends: |vim.Iter|
+
+    Special case implementations for iterators on list tables.
+
+    Fields: ~
+      • {flatten}  (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>, depth: integer?): vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:flatten()|.
+      • {rev}      (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>): vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:rev()|.
+      • {rfind}    (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>, f: V1|fun(v: V1, ...: V...): boolean): V1?, V...`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:rfind()|.
+      • {pop}      (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>): V1?, V...`) See
+                   |ArrayIter:pop()|.
+      • {rpeek}    (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>): V1?, V...`) See
+                   |ArrayIter:rpeek()|.
+      • {rskip}    (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>, n: integer): vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:rskip()|.
+      • {slice}    (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>, first: integer, last: integer): vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:slice()|.
+
+vim.Iter<V1, V...> *vim.Iter*
+    Array pipelines are a common special case that EmmyLua can model
+    precisely. General iterators can yield any number of values, so the public
+    type tracks a head value plus a generic tail pack.

comment_data.body: repeating the docs for ArrayIter and Iter feels bad. I know, they aren't exactly the same. but that's kind of pedantic. the "iter" interface is one iterface and they mostly are the same, and we can note differences where needed.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3009,16 +3017,226 @@ Examples: >lua
 <
 
 
+vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...> *vim.ArrayIter*
+    Extends: |vim.Iter|
+
+    Special case implementations for iterators on list tables.
+
+    Fields: ~
+      • {flatten}  (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>, depth: integer?): vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:flatten()|.
+      • {rev}      (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>): vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:rev()|.
+      • {rfind}    (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>, f: V1|fun(v: V1, ...: V...): boolean): V1?, V...`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:rfind()|.
+      • {pop}      (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>): V1?, V...`) See
+                   |ArrayIter:pop()|.
+      • {rpeek}    (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>): V1?, V...`) See
+                   |ArrayIter:rpeek()|.
+      • {rskip}    (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>, n: integer): vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:rskip()|.
+      • {slice}    (`fun(self: vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>, first: integer, last: integer): vim.ArrayIter<V1, V...>`)
+                   See |ArrayIter:slice()|.
+
+vim.Iter<V1, V...> *vim.Iter*
+    Array pipelines are a common special case that EmmyLua can model
+    precisely. General iterators can yield any number of values, so the public
+    type tracks a head value plus a generic tail pack.
+
+    Fields: ~
+      • {filter}     (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, f: fun(v: V1, ...: V...): boolean): vim.Iter<V1, V...>`)
+                     See |Iter:filter()|.
+      • {unique}     (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, key: fun(v: V1, ...: V...): any?): vim.Iter<V1, V...>`)
+                     See |Iter:unique()|.
+      • {map}        (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, f: fun(v: V1, ...: V...): K2, V2...): vim.Iter<K2, V2...>`)
+                     See |Iter:map()|.
+      • {each}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, f: fun(v: V1, ...: V...))`)
+                     See |Iter:each()|.
+      • {totable}    (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>): any[]`) See
+                     |Iter:totable()|.
+      • {join}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, delim: string): string`)
+                     See |Iter:join()|.
+      • {fold}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, init: A, f: fun(acc: A, v: V1, ...: V...): A): A`)
+                     See |Iter:fold()|.
+      • {next}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>): V1?, V...`) See
+                     |Iter:next()|.
+      • {peek}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>): V1?, V...`) See
+                     |Iter:peek()|.
+      • {find}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, f: (fun(v: V1, ...: V...): boolean)|any): V1?, V...`)
+                     See |Iter:find()|.
+      • {take}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, n: integer|fun(v: V1, ...: V...): boolean): vim.Iter<V1, V...>`)
+                     See |Iter:take()|.
+      • {skip}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, n: integer|fun(v: V1, ...: V...): boolean): vim.Iter<V1, V...>`)
+                     See |Iter:skip()|.
+      • {nth}        (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, n: integer): V1?, V...`)
+                     See |Iter:nth()|.
+      • {any}        (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, pred: fun(v: V1, ...: V...): boolean)`)
+                     See |Iter:any()|.
+      • {all}        (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>, pred: fun(v: V1, ...: V...): boolean)`)
+                     See |Iter:all()|.
+      • {last}       (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>): V1?, V...`) See
+                     |Iter:last()|.
+      • {enumerate}  (`fun(self: vim.Iter<V1, V...>): vim.Iter<integer, V1, V...>`)
+                     See |Iter:enumerate()|.
+
+
+ArrayIter:flatten({depth})                               *ArrayIter:flatten()*
+    Flattens a |list-iterator|, un-nesting nested values up to the given
+    {depth}. Errors if it attempts to flatten a dict-like value.

comment_data.body: trickery to make luals shut up.

comment_data.body:
Some comment leakage happened here, I'll tidy up.

I'm not sure how to handle this. We have a similar problem in lsp.txt. We do need a block to introduce the top level class and any members it has. Methods then get repeated so we get full documentation for params/returns etc.

I guess this sort of acts as an index.

comment_data.body: The "iter" interface is not one interface, it is strictly 2 disguised as 1, Iter and ArrayIter. They have different semantics and ArrayIter has quite a few more methods. This is my whole problem with the module. All I'm trying to do in this PR is get the typing working as best as possible. Getting it to work fully is impossible due to some of the weird semantics.

comment_data.body: I'm not really sure about this, it came out of the wash after changing the doc parsing scripts.

title: fix(vim.iter): document generic iterator types clearly
